content.text:
<user_query>
we're using the ec2 aws currently for the postgres, can we completely move the application to aws and not using the render and also for the domain using something free like .dev postfix?
</user_query>

Reviewing your current deployment setup and AWS docs so we can give concrete migration guidance.

[REDACTED]

Yes — you can move the whole app off Render onto AWS. You already have the hardest piece (Postgres on EC2 in `eu-central-1`). What’s left is running the Flask panel on that same box (or a second one) and putting HTTPS + a hostname in front of it.

## Current vs target

```text
Today:
  Render (Frankfurt) ──► EC2 Postgres (Frankfurt) :5432 public
  Local :5051 scrape ──► same Postgres

Target:
  Browser ──► EC2 (Frankfurt) :443 ──► gunicorn panel
                    └── localhost :5432 Postgres (private)
  Local scrape ──► Postgres (your IP only on 5432, or VPN/tunnel)
```

That’s a good fit for this repo: production already uses Docker + gunicorn (`Dockerfile`, `docker-entrypoint.sh`), and Render only runs the panel with `PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=0` — same as prod today.

## Recommended layout (solo / low cost)

**One EC2 `t4g.micro`** (the one you have):

| Service | How |
|--------|-----|
| Postgres | Keep Docker Postgres as today |
| Panel | Same image/command as Render: `docker-compose` or a second container |
| HTTPS | **Caddy** or **nginx** + Let’s Encrypt |
| Scrape | Still local (`PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1` on your laptop) |

**Why this works well**

- Panel and DB on one host → Postgres can bind to `127.0.0.1` only; you can **close public port 5432** (today it’s open for Render).
- You drop Render’s free-tier limits (512 MB RAM, cold starts, no fixed egress IP).
- After AWS free tier: roughly **~$8–10/mo** for one micro instance vs Render $0 + EC2 — net **cheaper** and simpler.

**Caveat:** `t4g.micro` (~1 GB RAM) is fine for the panel only. Heavy country scrapes with Playwright should stay on your machine, which matches how you run today.

## About a “free `.dev`” domain

**`.dev` is not free.** Google runs that TLD; registration is usually **~$12–15/year**, and browsers enforce HTTPS on `.dev`.

| Option | Cost | Notes |
|--------|------|--------|
| **`yourname.dev`** | ~$12/yr | Real domain; Let’s Encrypt works; professional |
| **DuckDNS** (`foo.duckdns.org`) | Free | Easy DDNS; fine for personal use |
| **sslip.io / nip.io** (`52.1.2.3.sslip.io`) | Free | No signup; ugly URL; OK for testing |
| **Cloudflare Tunnel** + any name | Free tunnel | HTTPS without opening 80/443; can use DuckDNS |

If you want zero domain cost, **DuckDNS + Caddy** or **Cloudflare Tunnel** are the usual choices. If you want a proper name, **`.dev` is cheap but not free**; **`.xyz`** is often ~$1–2 the first year.

## Migration steps (high level)

1. **v2 cutover** — Prod is still v1 on Render per project state; point deployment at v2 `scripts/panel_server.py` / existing Docker setup.
2. **On EC2** — Run the app container (same env as `render.yaml`: `DATABASE_URL`, `PANEL_SECRET_KEY`, admin creds, `PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=0`).
3. **Reverse proxy** — Caddy on 80/443 → `localhost:10000` (gunicorn).
4. **DNS** — Point hostname to the Elastic IP (you already have EIP tooling in `aws_postgres_migrate.sh`).
5. **Security group** — Open **22** (your IP), **80/443** (world); **remove 5432 from `0.0.0.0/0`**; allow 5432 only from your home IP for local scrape.
6. **Smoke test** — Login, board, job tracking (same checklist as `docs/operations/aws-postgres.md`).
7. **Decommission Render** — After a few days stable on AWS.

No app code changes are required for the move — mainly infra, env vars, and networking.

## Alternatives (if you outgrow one box)

| Approach | When |
|----------|------|
| **Second `t4g.micro` for app only** | DB and app compete for RAM |
| **RDS instead of Docker Postgres** | Less DB ops; ~$15–20/mo after free tier |
| **Lightsail $5 bundle** | Simpler than raw EC2; static IP included |
| **ECS/Fargate** | Overkill for this project |

## Risks to plan for

- **Backups** — You own them on EC2 (daily `pg_dump` cron is documented in `aws-postgres.md`).
- **Uptime** — No Render health checks unless you add them (Caddy + systemd, or UptimeRobot).
- **TLS renewal** — Caddy handles Let’s Encrypt automatically if 80/443 are reachable.
- **Scrape from laptop** — Update security group when your IP changes (`sync-sg` already exists).

---

**Bottom line:** Full AWS is reasonable and likely cheaper than Render + EC2 Postgres. Use a **paid `.dev`** if you want a clean URL (~$12/yr), or **DuckDNS / Cloudflare Tunnel** if you want $0 for the hostname.

If you want to proceed, I can sketch a concrete `docker-compose.yml` + Caddyfile for your existing EC2 (single-box, private Postgres, DuckDNS or `.dev`). Which do you prefer: free subdomain or buying a `.dev`?

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
is there any other free options for domain?
</user_query>

Here are practical **free hostname** options, grouped by how “real” they feel and how well they work with HTTPS on your EC2 box.

## Truly free (no domain purchase)

### 1. Dynamic DNS subdomains (best balance for a personal panel)

You get something like `relocation-jobs.duckdns.org` pointing at your Elastic IP.

| Service | Example | HTTPS | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|--------|
| [DuckDNS](https://www.duckdns.org) | `yoursite.duckdns.org` | Yes (Let’s Encrypt + Caddy) | Simple, popular, free |
| [No-IP](https://www.noip.com) | `yoursite.hopto.org` | Yes | Free tier needs confirm every ~30 days |
| [Dynu](https://www.dynu.com) | `yoursite.dynu.net` | Yes | Similar to No-IP |
| [FreeDNS (afraid.org)](https://freedns.afraid.org) | `yoursite.mooo.com` (many shared domains) | Yes | More setup; lots of weird parent domains |

**Fit for you:** DuckDNS or No-IP + Caddy on EC2 is the usual “free production URL” pattern.

---

### 2. IP-based DNS (zero signup)

| Service | Example |
|--------|---------|
| [sslip.io](https://sslip.io) | `52-12-34-56.sslip.io` |
| [nip.io](https://nip.io) | `52-12-34-56.nip.io` |

- **Pros:** No account, works immediately with your Elastic IP  
- **Cons:** Ugly URL, harder to remember, some cert tools are fussier  
- **HTTPS:** Usually works with Let’s Encrypt if the name resolves correctly

Good for **testing**; less nice as a bookmark you use daily.

---

### 3. Cloudflare Tunnel (free HTTPS, no open ports)

[Cloudflare Tunnel](https://developers.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-one/connections/connect-networks/) runs on EC2 and gives you a public HTTPS URL without opening 80/443 on the instance.

| Mode | URL | Cost |
|------|-----|------|
| Quick tunnel | `random-name.trycloudflare.com` | Free, **temporary** |
| Named tunnel + your hostname | `panel.duckdns.org` (or a domain you own) | Tunnel free; hostname still from DuckDNS or paid domain |

**Pros:** Strong security (no inbound web ports), automatic HTTPS  
**Cons:** Extra moving part; quick tunnels aren’t stable long-term

---

### 4. Tailscale Funnel (if you already use Tailscale)

Personal Tailscale can expose a service as `https://something.your-tailnet.ts.net`.

- **Pros:** Free HTTPS, very secure  
- **Cons:** Tailscale-specific; URL is long; best if you’re the only user

Fine for **solo private access**, not ideal as a “public app URL.”

---

### 5. Dev / tunnel tools (not real domains)

| Tool | Good for |
|------|----------|
| ngrok (free) | Temporary demos; URL changes |
| localtunnel | Same |
| bore, serveo | Same |

These are **not** stable production hostnames.

---

## “Free” top-level domains — mostly avoid

| Option | Reality |
|--------|---------|
| **Freenom** (.tk, .ml, .ga, …) | Effectively dead/unreliable; domains get revoked |
| **eu.org** | Free **subdomain** under `something.eu.org`, but application + approval |
| **GitHub Pages** (`user.github.io`) | Free, but **static sites only** — not your Flask app |

There isn’t a trustworthy **free `.com` / `.dev` / `.io`** for ongoing use.

---

## Cheapest “real” domain (not free, but worth knowing)

If you want a normal name later:

- **`.xyz`** — often ~$1–2 first year  
- **`.dev`** — ~$12–15/year (HTTPS required, which you want anyway)  
- **Cloudflare Registrar** — at-cost pricing, no markup

---

## What I’d pick for your setup

| Goal | Pick |
|------|------|
| **$0, stable, good enough** | **DuckDNS** + Elastic IP + **Caddy** (Let’s Encrypt) |
| **$0, max security, solo use** | **Cloudflare Tunnel** (+ DuckDNS if you want a fixed name) |
| **$0, quick test** | `YOUR-IP.sslip.io` |
| **Proper URL, low cost** | Buy **`.xyz`** or **`.dev`** once (~$1–15/yr) |

---

**Summary:** There’s no free `.dev`. The realistic free options are **free subdomains** (DuckDNS, No-IP, afraid.org), **IP wildcards** (sslip.io), or **tunnels** (Cloudflare / Tailscale). For a personal relocation-jobs panel on EC2, **DuckDNS + Caddy** is the simplest long-term free choice.

If you want, I can outline exact steps: DuckDNS → Elastic IP → security group → Caddy on the same EC2 as Postgres.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
ok we want to go through some cheap domain names what would be your suggestion?
</user_query>

Checking current cheap TLD pricing so recommendations stay accurate.

[REDACTED]

input.search_term: cheapest domain TLD 2025 2026 renewal price cloudflare porkbun
input.explanation: Get current cheap domain TLD pricing for accurate recommendations.

content.text:
For a personal-but-serious tool like this, I’d optimize for **low renewal price** and a name that’s easy to say over the phone — not the cheapest first-year promo.

## Where to buy

| Registrar | Why |
|-----------|-----|
| **[Cloudflare Registrar](https://www.cloudflare.com/products/registrar/)** | At-cost pricing, same renewal as year 1 (~**$10.44/yr** for `.com`) |
| **[Porkbun](https://porkbun.com)** | Same idea, slightly more TLDs, no Cloudflare lock-in (~**$11/yr** `.com`) |

Avoid GoDaddy/Hostinger-style **$2 first year → $20+ renewal** unless you’re sure you’ll transfer after year one.

Check availability on [instantdomainsearch.com](https://instantdomainsearch.com) — it shows multiple TLDs and registrars side by side.

---

## TLD picks (cheapest *sensible* options)

| TLD | Typical cost | Good for this app? |
|-----|--------------|-------------------|
| **`.com`** | ~$10–11/yr (flat) | **Best default** — credible, cheap long-term |
| **`.dev`** | ~$12/yr | Strong fit (HTTPS required anyway); reads “tool for builders” |
| **`.app`** | ~$12–14/yr | Same vibe as `.dev`, slightly more “product” |
| **`.xyz`** | ~$1–2 yr 1, ~$10–13 renewal | OK if the exact name is taken on `.com` |
| **`.site` / `.online`** | Cheap yr 1 | Often bad renewals (~$25–40) — skip unless you verify renewal |
| **`.io`** | ~$45–50/yr | Nice for tech, but **not cheap** |
| **`.jobs`** | Often $30+/yr | Thematic but poor value |

**My default recommendation:** a **`.com`** if you find a decent name; **`.dev`** as the runner-up (fits a Flask panel you’ll run on AWS).

---

## Naming directions (concrete ideas)

Your app is: **relocation/visa-sponsored backend & software jobs** in a few EU countries. Names that work well are **short, spellable, and not tied to one country**.

### 1. Descriptive + clear (safest)

| Name | Vibe |
|------|------|
| `relocjobs.com` / `.dev` | Direct, matches the project |
| `sponsorjobs.dev` | Emphasizes visa/sponsorship |
| `relojobs.com` | Shorter “relo” slang |
| `visarelo.dev` | Visa + relocation |
| `relocboard.dev` | “Board” matches your UI |

### 2. Action / outcome (memorable)

| Name | Vibe |
|------|------|
| `moveforwork.dev` | Clear purpose |
| `workabroad.dev` | Broad, friendly |
| `landabroad.dev` | Slightly more aspirational |
| `relocready.dev` | “Ready to relocate” |

### 3. EU-focused (matches DE/NL/UK/PT scope)

| Name | Vibe |
|------|------|
| `eurelojobs.dev` | Geographic without one country |
| `eusponsorjobs.com` | Very explicit |
| `reloc.eu` | **`.eu`** is ~€5–10/yr if available — niche but on-theme |

### 4. Minimal / brandable (if available)

| Name | Vibe |
|------|------|
| `relohq.dev` | Short hub for relocation search |
| `sponsormap.dev` | “Map” of sponsoring employers |
| `relocateboard.com` | Close to current “relocation-jobs” naming |

### 5. What I’d avoid

- **`relocate.me`** — taken (your data source brand)
- **Hyphens** (`relocation-jobs.com`) — fine technically, worse to say aloud
- **Country in the name** (`nljobs`, `germanyreloc`) — you support four countries
- **Ultra-cheap weird TLDs** (`.tk`, `.ml`) — unreliable
- **Long names** — you’ll type this in bookmarks and env vars often

---

## My top 5 picks for *your* project

If I were registering today, I’d try these in order:

1. **`relocjobs.dev`** — short, accurate, ~$12/yr, HTTPS-native  
2. **`relocjobs.com`** — same name, more “official” if available (~$10/yr)  
3. **`sponsorjobs.dev`** — highlights the visa/sponsorship angle  
4. **`reloboard.dev`** — matches the panel/board UX  
5. **`eurelojobs.com`** — clear EU scope without locking to one country  

Honorable mention: **`relocjobs.xyz`** only if `.com`/`.dev` are taken and you confirm renewal isn’t a trap.

---

## Quick decision rule

```text
Good .com under 12 letters, renewal ~$10?  → buy .com on Cloudflare
Only .dev available?                        → buy .dev (great for this stack)
Everything taken?                           → add prefix: getrelocjobs, myrelocjobs, aminrelocjobs
```

---

## After you pick a name

- Point DNS **A record** → your EC2 Elastic IP  
- Use **Cloudflare DNS** (free) even if you register at Porkbun — CDN + DDoS optional  
- **Caddy** on EC2 for Let’s Encrypt — works well with `.dev` and `.com`

If you share 2–3 names you like (or themes you prefer — professional vs casual vs EU-specific), I can narrow to a shortlist and suggest which TLD fits each best. I can also draft the DNS + Caddy setup for whichever name you choose.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
relocation-jobs.uk
with just 5 $ per year.
what do you think of it?
on cloudflare.
</user_query>

For a **personal panel at ~$5/year on Cloudflare**, `relocation-jobs.uk` is a **good buy** — I’d register it unless you care a lot about sounding “global.”

## Why it works

| Factor | Verdict |
|--------|---------|
| **Price** | Strong. ~$5/yr at Cloudflare is at-cost Nominet pricing — no promo trap, same renewal next year |
| **Name** | Clear and matches your existing `relocation-jobs` naming (Render, repo identity) |
| **Registrar** | Cloudflare is a solid pick: flat pricing, free WHOIS privacy, easy DNS |
| **TLD quality** | `.uk` is reputable — not a sketchy cheap gTLD like some `$1` first-year domains |
| **HTTPS** | Fine with Caddy/Let’s Encrypt on EC2, or Cloudflare proxy in front |

## The one real downside

**`.uk` can read as “UK-only jobs.”** Your app covers **Germany, Netherlands, UK, and Portugal**, so visitors might assume it’s just Britain.

That’s manageable if:
- it’s mostly **you + a few people you share it with**, or  
- you add a line on the login/board: *“EU relocation jobs — DE, NL, UK, PT”*

If you expect **strangers** to discover it and trust it as a multi-country tool, `.com` or `.dev` feels slightly more neutral — but you pay ~2× for that.

## Smaller nits (not dealbreakers)

- **Hyphen** — fine for bookmarks; a bit awkward to say aloud (“relocation hyphen jobs dot uk”).
- **Length** — long, but descriptive personal tools often keep long names.
- **Direct `.uk`** — `relocation-jobs.uk` is valid (not `co.uk`). That’s normal since Nominet opened second-level `.uk`.

## vs your other options

```text
relocation-jobs.uk @ $5/yr   → best value + clear name
relocjobs.dev @ ~$12/yr      → shorter, more “dev tool”, less country-coded
relocjobs.com @ ~$10/yr      → most neutral, if available
```

## Bottom line

**Yes — go for it** if you’re optimizing for **cheap + honest pricing + a name that matches the project**. The UK TLD is the only meaningful tradeoff, and for a private/small-audience tool that’s usually fine.

After you register:
1. **A record** → Elastic IP (or **CNAME** if you use a subdomain).  
2. **Cloudflare proxy** (orange cloud): optional — gives free CDN/DDoS; ensure SSL mode is **Full** if EC2 terminates TLS with Caddy.  
3. Or **DNS only** (grey cloud) and let **Caddy on EC2** handle Let’s Encrypt directly — simpler for a single Flask app.

If you want, I can outline the exact Cloudflare DNS records for your EC2 Elastic IP once you have the domain.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
is this free also on AWS because what we have currently on aws is totally free for one year.
</user_query>

**Short answer:** Running the panel on the **same EC2** you already use for Postgres can stay **~$0 on AWS for the first year** (same free tier). The **domain is not free** — `relocation-jobs.uk` on Cloudflare is about **$5/year**, separate from AWS.

## What’s free today (year 1)

Your setup is **Scenario A** from your docs: one **`t4g.micro`** in `eu-central-1` with Docker Postgres.

| Item | Year 1 (new AWS account) |
|------|---------------------------|
| **One EC2 `t4g.micro`** | Free — 750 hrs/month covers one instance 24/7 |
| **EBS** (~20 GB for Postgres) | Usually within 30 GB free tier |
| **Elastic IP** (attached to running instance) | Free |
| **Data transfer out** | First ~100 GB/month to internet is typically free |
| **Postgres in Docker on that box** | No extra AWS charge |

**Adding the Flask panel on that same instance** (second Docker container or gunicorn beside Postgres) does **not** require a second EC2. Same machine → **same free-tier usage**. You’re not doubling EC2 cost.

## What is **not** free

| Item | Cost |
|------|------|
| **`relocation-jobs.uk` on Cloudflare** | ~**$5/year** (registrar, not AWS) |
| **Render** | $0 today — you’d drop this when you move |
| **Second EC2** (app on one box, DB on another) | Would use more free tier or cost money sooner |
| **RDS** instead of Docker Postgres | Separate product; has its own free tier but you’re not using it |
| **Route 53** (if you hosted DNS on AWS) | ~$0.50/month per zone — **skip**; use Cloudflare DNS (free) |

## After year 1

Roughly **~$8–10/month** for one `t4g.micro` + small EBS volume — same as your Postgres doc already says. Still cheaper than Render ($0) + EC2, because you drop Render entirely.

## Practical picture

```text
Year 1:
  AWS (1× EC2: Postgres + panel)     ~$0
  Cloudflare domain relocation-jobs.uk  ~$5/year
  Render                                $0 → cancel after cutover

Year 2+:
  AWS EC2                               ~$8–10/month
  Domain                                ~$5/year
```

## One caveat

Free tier applies to **new AWS accounts for 12 months** from signup. If that year is almost over, plan for ~$8–10/month soon. Check in the AWS console: **Billing → Free tier** or **Cost Explorer**.

**Bottom line:** Yes — full app on **existing** EC2 can stay AWS-free in year 1. The only recurring cost you’re adding is the **~$5/year domain** on Cloudflare, not AWS.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
ok i will buy relocation-jobs.uk now from cloudflare and then come back to you.
</user_query>

Good plan — **`relocation-jobs.uk` on Cloudflare** is a solid choice at ~$5/year with honest renewals.

When you’re back, have these handy (or we can pull them from your setup):

1. **Elastic IP** of your EC2 Postgres box (from `aws-postgres.env` or `./scripts/aws_postgres_migrate.sh status`)
2. **Confirmation** the domain shows in your Cloudflare dashboard

Then we can wire it up end-to-end:

- Cloudflare DNS → your Elastic IP  
- HTTPS (Caddy on EC2, or Cloudflare proxy — we’ll pick one)  
- Run the panel container on the same EC2 as Postgres  
- Lock down Postgres (localhost only, close public `:5432`)  
- Cut over from Render and smoke-test

See you when the domain is registered.

[REDACTED]
